era_context: The mid-15th century continues with the Ottoman Empire at the height of its military power under Murad II, European states engaged in the final phases of the Hundred Years' War, and the Italian Renaissance flourishing. The Council of Constance's reforms are being implemented unevenly; the Ming Dynasty is consolidating its power in China; and Portuguese maritime exploration is accelerating.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asian and Steppe polities (sparse written records in European sources)
Pacific Islander societies (no contemporary written records)

events.title: Peace of Edirne (Ottoman-Hungarian truce)
events.description: The Ottoman Sultan Murad II and Hungarian military commander John Hunyadi negotiate a peace treaty at Edirne (Adrianople), establishing a truce between the Ottoman Empire and Hungary. The treaty is intended to be a lasting peace, but it proves short-lived; within a year, the agreement is broken, leading to the resumption of hostilities and ultimately the Battle of Varna in 1444. The treaty represents a moment of Ottoman-Christian negotiation and the temporary recognition of Ottoman power in the Balkans.
key_figures: Władysław III (King of Poland and Hungary)
events.certainty_note: The Peace of Edirne is well-attested in Ottoman, Hungarian, and Venetian sources. The date is firmly established as 1443.

events.title: Skanderbeg's rebellion against Ottoman rule
events.description: Gjergj Kastrioti, known as Skanderbeg, a former Ottoman officer, leads a rebellion against Ottoman rule in Albania. He declares himself the leader of the Albanian resistance and begins a 25-year struggle for Albanian independence. Skanderbeg's rebellion is one of the most significant Christian resistances to Ottoman expansion in the Balkans, though ultimately unsuccessful in preventing Ottoman control. His military tactics and leadership inspire later resistance movements and make him a legendary figure in Albanian national identity.
events.certainty_note: Skanderbeg's rebellion is well-attested in Ottoman and Venetian sources. The date of 1443 is traditionally given for the beginning of his revolt, though some sources place it slightly later.

events.title: Council of Florence convenes
events.description: The Council of Florence (also called the Council of Union) convenes under Pope Eugenius IV, bringing together Eastern Orthodox and Roman Catholic representatives to attempt reconciliation of the Great Schism of 1054. The council includes Byzantine Emperor John VIII Palaiologus and Patriarch Joseph II of Constantinople. The council results in the Decree of Union (Laetentur Caeli) in 1439, though the union proves short-lived and is repudiated by the Eastern Orthodox Church after the Fall of Constantinople in 1453.

sources.name: Council of Florence records and decrees

events.certainty_note: The Council of Florence is well-documented in papal and council records. The council convened in 1438 and continued through 1445; 1443 marks a significant phase of negotiations.
cross_references: Followed by 1453-001 (Fall of Constantinople, union repudiated)

events.title: Afonso V becomes King of Portugal
events.description: Afonso V, known as 'Afonso the African,' becomes King of Portugal at age six following the death of his father, Edward of Portugal. His reign is marked by continued Portuguese expansion in North Africa and the Atlantic, including the conquest of Tangier and the continuation of Prince Henry the Navigator's exploration programs. Afonso V's reign sees the acceleration of Portuguese colonial ambitions and the beginning of the Portuguese slave trade.
key_figures: Prince Henry the Navigator (uncle, regent and patron of exploration)

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery of Guinea

events.certainty_note: Afonso V's accession is well-documented in Portuguese royal records. The date of 1443 is firmly established.

events.title: Inca expansion under Pachacuti
events.description: Pachacuti Inca Yupanqui, the ninth Inca emperor, continues his military campaigns to expand the Inca Empire. By 1443, he has already defeated the Chancas and is consolidating control over the Andean region. His reign marks the beginning of the Inca Empire's rapid expansion, which will eventually create the largest pre-Columbian state in the Americas. Pachacuti's military innovations and administrative reforms transform the Inca state into a centralised empire.

sources.name: Quipucamayoc (Inca oral tradition recorded by Spanish chroniclers)
Pedro Cieza de León, Chronicle of Peru
Felipe Guaman Poma de Ayala, Nueva Corónica y Buen Gobierno

events.certainty_note: Inca chronology is notoriously uncertain, as the Inca did not use a written calendar system. The date of 1443 is assigned by modern scholars based on Spanish colonial sources and oral traditions recorded centuries after the events. The actual date could vary by several decades.
cross_references: Led to 1532-001 (Spanish conquest of the Inca Empire)

disconfirming_evidence: The Peace of Edirne is sometimes portrayed as a definitive peace, but it was broken within a year, suggesting that both sides may have viewed it as a temporary arrangement rather than a lasting settlement. Skanderbeg's rebellion, while celebrated in later Albanian nationalism, was not as militarily successful as sometimes portrayed; Ottoman control of Albania was never seriously threatened. The Council of Florence's Decree of Union is often presented as a triumph of Catholic diplomacy, but the union was repudiated almost immediately by the Eastern Orthodox Church and had little practical effect. Afonso V's reign is sometimes romanticised as an age of exploration, but Portuguese expansion was driven primarily by economic motives (gold, slaves) rather than the noble ideals often attributed to it. Pachacuti's dates are highly uncertain; some scholars place his reign earlier or later, and the extent of his conquests by 1443 is debated.
historiographic_note: The year 1443 is well-documented in European sources (Ottoman, Hungarian, Italian, Portuguese, papal records) but less thoroughly in Asian sources available to Western scholarship. The Council of Florence is extensively recorded in papal and council documents, but Byzantine perspectives are filtered through later compilations. African and American perspectives on Portuguese and Inca expansion are almost entirely absent from contemporary written sources; these regions are reconstructed from later oral traditions, archaeological evidence, and colonial-era accounts. The historiography of this period is heavily shaped by European and Ottoman state records; the experiences of common people, women, and non-elite groups are largely invisible. Inca chronology is particularly problematic, as the Inca did not use a written calendar system, and Spanish colonial sources are often contradictory.

graph_edges.note: The Peace of Edirne was broken within a year, leading to the resumption of hostilities and the Battle of Varna in 1444.
Skanderbeg's rebellion, which began in 1443, continued for 25 years until his death in 1468.
The Council of Florence's attempt at reconciliation was undermined by the Fall of Constantinople in 1453, which the Eastern Orthodox Church blamed on Catholic betrayal.
Afonso V's accession in 1443 initiated a reign marked by continued Portuguese expansion, including the conquest of Tangier in 1471.
Pachacuti's expansion of the Inca Empire in 1443 created the state that would be conquered by Spanish conquistadors in 1532.
